evidence: typed-messages/ subdirectory uses PascalCase filenames (ToolCallCard.tsx, ArtifactRenderer.tsx, etc.) while all other component directories use kebab-case (message-input.tsx, channel-header.tsx, etc.)
MessageMetadata.tsx in chat/ is PascalCase while its 17 sibling files are kebab-case
packages/web/lib/ has 3 plain .js files (route-handlers.js, api-safety.js, validation.js) alongside 19 .ts files
overview-section.tsx and server-settings-overlay.tsx do not use useCallback while 6/8 sibling server-settings files do, using inline function declarations instead
use-channel.ts and use-dm-channel.ts both define an identical compareSequences utility function locally rather than sharing it
use-panel-state.ts exports both a hook (usePanelState) and multiple standalone utility functions (hydrateSavedPanel, reopenExistingPanel, etc.) while sibling hooks export only a single hook each

strengths: Named exports used consistently across all component files — zero default exports found in the components directory
kebab-case file naming is followed consistently across layout/, modals/, server-settings/, workspace/, and dm/ directories
Hooks directory follows a consistent use-*.ts naming pattern for all 6 hook files
All component files consistently use 'use client' directive and follow the same import ordering pattern (React, then Next.js, then local)
convention_outlier.issue_character: Convention drift is localized to a few specific areas rather than being systemic: a PascalCase filename island in typed-messages/, a .js/.ts split in lib/, and a duplicated utility function between two closely related hooks.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase has strong baseline conventions — named exports, kebab-case filenames, consistent hook naming — that a developer can learn once and largely trust. However, the typed-messages/ PascalCase island, the MessageMetadata.tsx outlier, and the .js files in a TypeScript lib/ directory create noticeable friction points where a developer would encounter 'why is this different?' moments. The duplicated compareSequences function between use-channel.ts and use-dm-channel.ts indicates that sibling modules are evolving independently without extracting shared utilities. These are real but bounded issues, placing the score solidly in the 'uneven' range below 80.

issues.summary: typed-messages/ uses PascalCase filenames while all other component directories use kebab-case
evidence: All 6 component files in typed-messages/ use PascalCase (ToolCallCard.tsx, ArtifactRenderer.tsx, etc.)
Every other component directory (layout/, modals/, server-settings/, workspace/, dm/, chat/ parent) uses kebab-case consistently
The barrel file typed-messages/index.ts re-exports from these PascalCase files, making the inconsistency visible in imports
issues.suggestion: Rename the 6 PascalCase files in typed-messages/ to kebab-case (tool-call-card.tsx, artifact-renderer.tsx, etc.) and update the barrel index.ts imports to match. This aligns with the codebase-wide kebab-case convention.

issues.summary: MessageMetadata.tsx uses PascalCase while all 17 sibling files in chat/ use kebab-case
evidence: MessageMetadata.tsx is PascalCase; all 17 siblings (message-input.tsx, channel-header.tsx, typing-indicator.tsx, etc.) are kebab-case
issues.suggestion: Rename MessageMetadata.tsx to message-metadata.tsx and update its importers.

issues.summary: Three .js files in packages/web/lib/ alongside 19 .ts files creates a mixed-language island
evidence: route-handlers.js, api-safety.js, and validation.js are plain JavaScript with no type annotations
Their 19 sibling files in lib/ are all TypeScript (.ts)
route-handlers.js uses untyped function parameters (e.g., isAuthorType(value)) unlike the typed patterns in neighboring .ts files
issues.suggestion: Convert the 3 .js files to .ts with proper type annotations to match the rest of the lib/ directory. This eliminates the style island and brings these server-side utilities under TypeScript's type checking.

issues.summary: compareSequences utility function is identically defined in both use-channel.ts and use-dm-channel.ts
evidence: use-channel.ts line 59-64: function compareSequences(a: string, b: string): number { ... }
use-dm-channel.ts line 27-32: identical function compareSequences(a: string, b: string): number { ... }
issues.suggestion: Extract compareSequences into a shared utility (e.g., packages/web/lib/sequence.ts) and import it in both hooks. This removes the duplication and ensures both hooks stay in sync if the comparison logic changes.

root_causes: The typed-messages/ directory was likely added in a single batch by a contributor who preferred PascalCase component filenames, and no linting rule enforces filename convention
The 3 .js files in lib/ appear to be from an early development phase before the project fully committed to TypeScript — an incomplete migration artifact
possible_false_positives: The sibling_behavior signals about missing useCallback in overview-section.tsx and server-settings-overlay.tsx — these are simpler components that may not need useCallback since their handler functions are not passed as props to memoized children
